title: When money arrives, what do I do?
subtitle: A fifteen-minute journal prompt for your instinctive response to receiving money.
excerpt: A fifteen-minute journal prompt from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program. The prompt maps your instinctive response when money arrives, the relief, the impulse to spend it before it leaves, the bracing for the gap that follows. Use when money is flowing and the feast-or-famine pattern is visible.
sourceNotes: From Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). Journal-prompt practice as a self-inquiry tool is a public-domain technique documented across therapeutic and journaling traditions.

content.text: A fifteen-minute journal prompt from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program. The prompt maps your instinctive response when money arrives, the first feeling when the transfer confirms, the impulse that follows, what the body does before the thinking catches up. Use it in a flush period, when a payment has just landed and the usual pattern is visible.
New to this style of journal practice? Read
When I receive money, how do I usually react?

What is the first feeling when money arrives? What is the next impulse, to spend it quickly, to protect it, to feel relief before bracing for the gap? Does receiving money feel safe, or does it feel temporary? Write without editing. Ten to fifteen minutes.
If you have more time: what would it feel like to receive money and simply hold it, no impulse to spend or protect or brace? What's in the way of that response? Write that gap.

Close the notebook. One slow breath. If you spotted a pattern you hadn't named before, such as the spend-it-before-it-goes impulse or the bracing right after relief, mark the page. That pattern is the thing the tapping and energy statement work is aimed at.

From Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). The focus there is ending the feast-or-famine cycle. Using journal prompts to inquire is a public-domain technique. It shows up across therapy and journaling traditions.
